questions.q: Gaussian elimination with partial pivoting chooses pivot elements by selecting the largest absolute value in each column. Why is this important?
options: To minimize rounding errors and prevent division by small numbers that amplify perturbations
To ensure the matrix is invertible
To reduce the number of arithmetic operations
To guarantee the solution is unique
questions.explanation: Partial pivoting reduces growth of rounding errors during elimination. Dividing by a small pivot magnifies any floating-point errors. Choosing the largest |pivot| controls error propagation. Full pivoting (searching rows and columns) is even better but more expensive.

questions.q: LU decomposition factors a matrix A as A = LU where L is lower triangular and U is upper triangular. What is a major computational advantage?
options: Once A is factored, solving Ax = b for multiple right-hand sides requires only O(n²) operations per solve
LU factorization requires fewer operations than Gaussian elimination
LU decomposition automatically computes the determinant
It eliminates the need for pivoting strategies
questions.explanation: LU factorization costs O(n³) once. After factoring A = LU, solving Ax = b = LUx requires forward substitution (L → y) and back substitution (U → x), each O(n²). So multiple right-hand sides amortize the factorization cost.

questions.q: The condition number of a matrix A, denoted κ(A), relates perturbations in A and b to perturbations in the solution x. If κ(A) = 10^8, the system is called:
options: Ill-conditioned; small errors in input grow by factor ≈10^8
Well-conditioned; easy to solve accurately
Singular; the matrix has determinant exactly zero
Over-determined; it has more equations than unknowns
questions.explanation: High condition number (κ >> 1) indicates an ill-conditioned system. Relative error in x is amplified by κ: δx/‖x‖ ≈ κ(A) · (δA/‖A‖ + δb/‖b‖). For κ = 10^8 with single-precision (≈10^{-7} error), solutions lose about 8 decimal digits of significance.

questions.q: Iterative methods like Jacobi and Gauss-Seidel solve Ax = b by fixed-point iteration x^{(k+1)} = Gx^{(k)} + c. When do they converge?
options: When the spectral radius ρ(G) < 1, i.e., the largest eigenvalue magnitude of G is less than 1
Only if A is symmetric
When the matrix is diagonally dominant
Always, for any non-singular A
questions.explanation: Convergence requires ρ(G) < 1. Gauss-Seidel converges for diagonally dominant or symmetric positive-definite matrices. Jacobi is slower, converging for diagonally dominant A. The spectral radius determines convergence rate: e^{(k)} ≈ ρ(G)^k · e^{(0)}.

questions.q: Gauss-Seidel iteration typically converges faster than Jacobi because it:
options: Uses updated values immediately in the same iteration rather than waiting for the next iteration
Requires fewer arithmetic operations per step
Automatically selects optimal relaxation parameters
Avoids storing the matrix A
questions.explanation: In Jacobi, x_i^{(k+1)} uses all x_j^{(k)}. In Gauss-Seidel, x_i^{(k+1)} uses newly computed x_j^{(k+1)} for j < i and x_j^{(k)} for j > i. This asymmetry accelerates convergence, reducing the spectral radius ρ(G).

questions.q: For a symmetric positive-definite (SPD) matrix A, which statement is true about its condition number?
options: κ(A) = λ_max/λ_min, the ratio of the largest to smallest eigenvalue
κ(A) is always equal to the matrix rank
κ(A) determines the determinant of A
κ(A) = 1 for all SPD matrices
questions.explanation: For SPD matrices with eigenvalues 0 < λ_min ≤ ... ≤ λ_max, the 2-norm condition number is κ₂(A) = λ_max / λ_min. Well-conditioned SPD matrices have λ values close together; ill-conditioned ones have large spread.

questions.q: The conjugate gradient (CG) method solves SPD systems Ax = b without explicitly forming A. What is its main advantage over Gaussian elimination?
options: For sparse A, it requires only O(n²) operations and avoids fill-in; it is matrix-free
It always converges in one iteration
It requires no initial guess
It automatically computes the condition number
questions.explanation: CG is ideal for large sparse systems because it uses only matrix-vector products (A·v), avoiding fill-in and storage of dense factors. It converges in at most n iterations for exact arithmetic, but convergence is often achieved much faster for well-conditioned systems.

questions.q: Preconditioning a linear system Ax = b transforms it to Px^{−1}Ax = P^{−1}b, where P ≈ A is invertible. Why?
options: To reduce the condition number of the system, accelerating iterative solver convergence
To make the matrix A symmetric
To eliminate the need for an initial guess in iterative methods
To guarantee convergence of Gaussian elimination
questions.explanation: Preconditioning with P such that κ(P^{−1}A) << κ(A) dramatically improves convergence of iterative methods like CG or GMRES. Common preconditioners: Jacobi (diagonal scaling), ILU (incomplete LU), or multigrid. Better conditioning → fewer iterations.

questions.q: A diagonally dominant matrix satisfies |a_{ii}| > Σ_{j≠i} |a_{ij}| for each row. Why do Jacobi and Gauss-Seidel converge for such matrices?
options: Diagonal dominance ensures ρ(G) < 1 for the splitting matrix G, guaranteeing convergence
It makes the matrix symmetric
It ensures the matrix has all positive eigenvalues
It allows skipping pivoting in Gaussian elimination
questions.explanation: Diagonal dominance implies the iteration matrix G = D^{−1}(D - A) (Jacobi) satisfies ρ(G) < 1. This is because the diagonal element dominates, making perturbations shrink per iteration. For Gauss-Seidel, ρ(G) is even smaller.

questions.q: When solving Ax = b with A nearly singular (near-zero eigenvalues), Tikhonov regularization minimizes ‖Ax - b‖² + λ‖x‖². What does the parameter λ control?
options: The trade-off between data fit and solution smoothness; larger λ favors smaller x
The number of iterations in an iterative solver
The accuracy of the LU factorization
The pivoting strategy in Gaussian elimination
questions.explanation: Tikhonov regularization solves the damped normal equations (A^TA + λI)x = A^Tb. The parameter λ balances residual minimization (first term) against solution norm (second term). Larger λ biases x toward zero, stabilizing the solution for ill-posed problems.
